g.data-narration: Welcome to Java Collections Framework - the backbone of data handling in Java applications.
The Collections Framework provides ready-to-use data structures, saving you from building complex algorithms from scratch.
g.text: • Reusable data structures: ArrayList, HashSet, HashMap, etc.
• Performance optimized: No need to reinvent the wheel
• Unified architecture: Consistent API across all collections

g.data-narration: The framework defines six core interfaces: List, Set, Queue, Deque, Map, and the root Collection interface.
A List is an ordered collection that allows duplicates and provides index-based access to elements.
g.text: An ordered collection (sequence) with these key properties:

g.data-narration: ArrayList is the most common List implementation. Creating one is simple using the new keyword with generic type.
Use the add() method to insert elements. They are placed in order - first added goes to index 0, second to index 1, and so on.
Lists allow duplicates. Adding 'Ananya' again places another copy at the next available index, creating [Ananya, Bharat, Chitra, Ananya].
To retrieve an element, use get(index). Indexing starts at 0, so get(1) returns the second element - 'Bharat'.
Internally, ArrayList uses a dynamic array that automatically resizes. When full, it creates a larger array and copies elements.
g.text: Think of ArrayList as a resizable array - elements stored in contiguous memory locations.

g.data-narration: ArrayList provides O(1) constant time for random access but O(n) for insertion/deletion in the middle due to element shifting.
g.text: Understanding time complexity helps you choose the right collection:

g.data-narration: ArrayList provides many methods: add, get, remove, set, contains, size, isEmpty, clear - all essential for data manipulation.
In summary: Lists maintain insertion order, allow duplicates, and provide fast random access through zero-based indexing.
In real applications, Lists store product catalogs, user activity logs, menu items, queue management, and much more.
